text: The patient is a sixty-one year old lady with an history of repeated kidney transplantation, parathyroidectomy for adenoma, who underwent total abdominal hysterectomy and bilateral salpingophorectomy with lymphadenectomy in two thousand sixteen.
In twenty eighteen she was diagnosed with the rectovaginal fistula.
The decision of a multidisciplinary committee was to perform a diverting colostomy to delay repair after patient optimization.
After an initial improvement, symptoms worsened.
An MRI scan in July twenty nineteen confirmed the rectovaginal fistula.
The patient is positioned in a prone jackknife position with legs open, preparing for the surgical procedure.
The robotic surgical system is positioned on the right side of the patient, ready to assist in the ampullectomy procedure.
The robotic port is placed on the jet point path, visible on the right side of the patient positioned in a prone jackknife position.
An air seal port is visible, facilitating the maintenance of pneumoperitoneum during the procedure.
The surgical setup involves three robotic arms positioned for the ampullectomy procedure, visible in the operating field.
The assistant surgeon can sit between the legs of the patient.
The surgeon begins the procedure by separating the rectum from the vagina, using precise dissection techniques to carefully distinguish the anatomical boundaries between these structures.
The orifice is visible in blue, indicating its location within the surgical field.
The surgeon uses diathermy to prepare the rectovaginal septum around the orifice, creating a clear area for subsequent suturing.
The surgical team continues the procedure in a cephalad direction, progressing upwards from the previously prepared rectovaginal septum.
The surgeon uses robotic bipolar forceps to gently lift the rectal wall, providing a clear view of the underlying tissue layers.
The surgeon develops the dissection plane laterally, carefully dissecting the distal portion to separate the rectum from the vagina.
The surgeon displays the orifice in the vagina, highlighted in red. Single stitches are used to repair and close the vaginal wall.
The surgeon completes the suturing of the rectovaginal septum, securing the tissue around the orifice with single stitches.
The surgeon places a biologic mesh over the rectum and begins the repair process, ensuring the mesh is properly positioned to support the rectal wall.
A biologic mesh is placed over the vaginal suture, covering the repaired area of the vaginal wall.
The surgeon uses a V-lock stitch to close the rectum with a running suture, ensuring the tissue edges are approximated securely.
The final result of the surgical procedure is displayed, showing the completed repair with the biologic mesh covering the vaginal wall and the rectum closed with a V-lock stitch.
The patient was discharged on postoperative day three after undergoing planned hemodialysis.
